options.option: Inline is fastest, external is slowest
Inline applies to one element, internal applies to one page, external applies across multiple pages
Inline requires <script>, internal requires <link>, external requires <style>

font-family

questions.question: If two conflicting CSS rules apply to the same element, which one takes priority?

The one written last in the stylesheet

questions.question: What is the difference between relative units (em, %) and absolute units (px, pt)?

options.option: Relative units scale based on context, absolute units are fixed sizes
Relative units are always bigger than absolute units
Relative units only work in mobile, absolute units only work in desktop
No difference

Padding

Space inside the border and around the content

box-sizing
border-box

content-box

padding
border

options.option: Margins collapse vertically but not horizontally
Margins always collapse both vertically and horizontally
Margins never collapse

The one declared first in HTML

questions.question: Which of the following properties is not affected by box-sizing?

padding

questions.question: What is the main difference between relative and absolute positioning?

options.option: Both remove the element from flow
Relative stays in flow, absolute doesn’t
Absolute shifts relative to itself

options.option: static
absolute
sticky

Only border

options.option: Top-left of the viewport always
Top-left of its positioned ancestor
Top-left of body only

questions.question: Which combination ensures an element’s box doesn’t exceed a fixed width regardless of border or padding?

options.option: width + margin: auto
width + display: block
width + box-sizing: border-box
width + overflow: hidden

questions.question: What CSS property turns an element into a flex container?

options.option: display: flex
options.comment: Setting display: flex on a parent element activates flexbox, making its direct children flex items.

options.option: flex: container
position: flex
layout: flex

questions.question: What is the default value of flex-direction on a flex container?

options.option: row
options.comment: flex-direction defaults to row, placing items horizontally left-to-right (in LTR writing mode).

options.option: column
row-reverse
column-reverse

questions.question: Which CSS property controls how flex items are distributed along the main axis?

options.option: justify-content
options.comment: justify-content controls spacing and alignment on the main axis. align-items handles the cross axis.

options.option: align-items
align-content
flex-align

questions.question: What is the default value of the flex-wrap property?

options.option: nowrap
options.comment: flex-wrap defaults to nowrap, so all items stay on one line even if they overflow the container.

options.option: wrap
wrap-reverse
auto

questions.question: Which value of justify-content places equal space between items but no space at the container edges?

options.option: space-between
options.comment: space-between distributes remaining space only between items. The first item sits at the start edge and the last at the end edge.

options.option: space-around
space-evenly
center

questions.question: What is the cross axis in a flex container with flex-direction: row?

options.option: The vertical axis
options.comment: The main axis runs in the direction of flex-direction. For row that is horizontal, so the perpendicular cross axis is vertical.

options.option: The horizontal axis
The z-axis
Whichever axis is longer

questions.question: What does align-items: center do on a flex container?

options.option: Centers flex items along the cross axis
options.comment: align-items controls cross-axis alignment. center vertically centers items when flex-direction is row.

options.option: Centers flex items along the main axis
Centers the flex container on the page
Centers text inside each flex item

questions.question: What does flex-grow: 1 mean on a flex item?

options.option: The item will expand to fill any remaining space in the container
options.comment: flex-grow sets the growth factor. A value of 1 means the item participates in distributing available space. If all siblings have flex-grow: 1, space is shared equally.

options.option: The item is exactly 1px wide
The item has a minimum size of 1rem
The item cannot shrink

questions.question: When does align-content have any effect on a flex container?

options.option: When flex-wrap is enabled and there are multiple lines of items
options.comment: align-content controls spacing between flex lines. With the default nowrap there is only one line, so align-content has no effect.

options.option: Whenever there is more than one flex item
Only when flex-direction is column
Only when align-items is also set on the container

questions.question: What does the order property control on a flex item?

options.option: The visual rendering position within the flex container
options.comment: Items are rendered in ascending order value. Default is 0 for all items, so ties maintain DOM order. order only changes visual layout, not DOM or accessibility tree order.

options.option: The z-index of the item relative to siblings
The number of columns the item spans
The size of the item relative to its siblings

questions.question: The shorthand flex: 1 is equivalent to which longhand declaration?

options.option: flex-grow: 1; flex-shrink: 1; flex-basis: 0%
options.comment: A single unitless number in the flex shorthand sets flex-grow and resets flex-shrink to 1 and flex-basis to 0% — different from the initial flex-basis: auto.

options.option: flex-grow: 1; flex-shrink: 0; flex-basis: auto
flex-grow: 1; flex-shrink: 1; flex-basis: auto
flex-grow: 0; flex-shrink: 1; flex-basis: 1%

questions.question: How does flex-direction: column change a flex container?

options.option: The main axis becomes vertical, stacking items top-to-bottom
options.comment: flex-direction: column rotates the main axis 90 degrees. justify-content now controls vertical spacing and align-items controls horizontal alignment.

options.option: Items are placed right-to-left
Items are arranged diagonally
justify-content stops having any effect

questions.question: What does align-self do on a flex item?

options.option: It overrides the container's align-items value for that individual item
options.comment: align-self accepts the same values as align-items but applies to a single item, letting it deviate from the container-level cross-axis alignment.

options.option: It aligns all items in the container simultaneously
It positions the item on the main axis
It removes the item from the normal flex flow

questions.question: Which justify-content value places equal space between items AND equal space between the first/last item and the container edges?

options.option: space-evenly
options.comment: space-evenly distributes remaining space so every gap — between any two items and between items and the edges — is identical.

options.option: space-around
space-between
stretch

questions.question: What does flex: 0 0 200px mean for a flex item?

options.option: The item has a fixed size of 200px — it will not grow or shrink
options.comment: flex: 0 0 200px sets flex-grow: 0 (no growth), flex-shrink: 0 (no shrinking), flex-basis: 200px. The item is rigidly 200px wide regardless of available space.

options.option: The item starts at 200px and can grow but not shrink
The item is invisible and reserves 200px of space
The item grows by 200 units for every available pixel

questions.question: What is the key difference between flex-basis: auto and flex-basis: 0?

options.option: auto uses the item's content size as the starting size; 0 treats the content size as zero
options.comment: With flex-basis: auto the item's content width is the base before free space is distributed. With 0, all space is treated as free space, so items grow in proportion to their flex-grow ratios alone.

options.option: auto means the item does not flex; 0 means it flexes to fill everything
They behave identically in all modern browsers
auto applies only to width; 0 applies only to height

questions.question: A flex item has flex-shrink: 0. What happens when the container is too small to fit all items?

options.option: The item will not shrink and will overflow the container if necessary
options.comment: flex-shrink: 0 opts the item out of the shrink algorithm. Sibling items with flex-shrink: 1 will absorb the deficit, but this item stays at its flex-basis size and may overflow.

options.option: The item is removed from the layout automatically
The overflow is hidden by the browser
The item shrinks proportionally like all other items

questions.question: You want a flex item to fill all remaining space but never shrink below its natural content size. Which declaration achieves this?

options.option: flex: 1 0 auto
options.comment: flex: 1 0 auto means flex-grow: 1 (fill available space), flex-shrink: 0 (never shrink), flex-basis: auto (start from natural content size). The item grows freely but its floor is its content width.

options.option: flex: 0 1 auto
flex: 1 1 0
flex: 0 0 auto

questions.question: In a flex container, gap: 16px 8px is applied. How is this interpreted?

options.option: 16px row gap (between wrapped lines) and 8px column gap (between items within a line)
options.comment: The gap shorthand follows the row-gap / column-gap order. In a row-direction flex container the column-gap is between items and row-gap is between wrapped lines.

options.option: 16px column gap and 8px row gap
16px outer margin and 8px inner padding per item
16px gap on the main axis and 8px on the cross axis always

questions.question: In a multi-line flex container (flex-wrap: wrap), what is the difference between align-items and align-content?

options.option: align-items aligns items within each line; align-content spaces the lines relative to each other
options.comment: align-items controls per-line cross-axis alignment (how items sit within their row). align-content controls how the flex lines are spaced relative to each other and the container.

options.option: align-items works on single-line containers only; align-content on multi-line only
They are the same property with different names
align-content aligns on the main axis; align-items aligns on the cross axis

questions.question: What CSS property turns an element into a grid container?

options.option: display: grid
options.comment: Setting display: grid on a parent element activates CSS Grid, making its direct children grid items placed into a grid.

options.option: grid: container
position: grid
layout: grid

questions.question: Which property defines the column sizes in a CSS Grid container?

options.option: grid-template-columns
options.comment: grid-template-columns sets the number and size of columns. e.g. grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr creates three equal columns.

options.option: grid-columns
column-template
grid-column-count

questions.question: What does the fr unit represent in CSS Grid?

options.option: A fraction of the available free space in the grid container
options.comment: fr stands for fraction. 1fr means one part of the remaining space after fixed-size tracks are placed. 1fr 2fr gives one-third and two-thirds of available space.

options.option: A fixed pixel value equal to 1% of the viewport
A font-relative length unit
A frame rate unit for animations

questions.question: How do you create gaps between grid rows and columns?

options.option: Using the gap (or row-gap / column-gap) property on the grid container
options.comment: gap: 16px sets equal gaps between all rows and columns. You can also use row-gap and column-gap independently for different spacing.

options.option: Using margin on each grid item
Using the grid-spacing property
Gaps are not possible in CSS Grid

questions.question: What does grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr) create?

options.option: Three equal-width columns that share available space
options.comment: repeat(3, 1fr) is shorthand for 1fr 1fr 1fr. The three columns each get one-third of the available container width.

options.option: Three columns each 1 pixel wide
A grid with rows repeated 3 times
A single column with 3fr of space

questions.question: What is the key difference between CSS Grid and Flexbox?

options.option: Grid is two-dimensional (rows and columns); Flexbox is one-dimensional (row or column)
options.comment: CSS Grid lets you control layout in both axes simultaneously. Flexbox is designed for single-axis layouts — either a row or a column, but not both at once.

options.option: Grid only works for images; Flexbox works for all elements
Flexbox uses fr units; Grid uses percentages only
Grid requires JavaScript; Flexbox is pure CSS

questions.question: What does grid-column: span 2 do on a grid item?

options.option: The item spans across 2 column tracks
options.comment: grid-column: span 2 tells the item to occupy 2 consecutive column tracks starting from its auto-placed position.

options.option: The item is placed in the second column only
The item is given a width of 2fr
The item creates 2 new columns

questions.question: What does the grid-template-areas property allow you to do?

options.option: Name regions of the grid and assign items to those named areas
options.comment: grid-template-areas lets you draw the layout as ASCII art using names. Items are then placed with grid-area: name, making layouts highly readable.

options.option: Define the number of rows in the grid
Set the background color for grid cells
Create fixed-size tracks for specific breakpoints

questions.question: What is the difference between explicit and implicit grid tracks?

options.option: Explicit tracks are defined by grid-template-*; implicit are auto-created for overflow items
options.comment: When items exceed the defined tracks, the browser auto-generates implicit tracks. grid-auto-rows and grid-auto-columns control the size of these implicit tracks.

options.option: Explicit tracks are visible; implicit tracks are hidden
Implicit tracks use fr units; explicit tracks use pixels
They are the same — the terms are interchangeable

questions.question: What does place-items: center do on a grid container?

options.option: Centers each grid item both horizontally and vertically within its cell
options.comment: place-items is shorthand for align-items and justify-items. Setting it to center centers items on both axes within their grid area.

options.option: Centers the entire grid inside its parent
Centers only the grid columns
Creates a single centered column in the grid

questions.question: What does minmax(100px, 1fr) do when used in grid-template-columns?

options.option: Creates a column that is at least 100px wide but can grow to fill available space
options.comment: minmax(min, max) sets the lower and upper size bounds for a track. minmax(100px, 1fr) means the column will never be smaller than 100px and will flex up to its share of free space.

options.option: Creates exactly 100 columns with 1fr each
Sets a column to exactly 100px and prevents resizing
Creates a minimum of 1 column and a maximum of 100px total width

questions.question: How do you place a grid item so it starts at column line 2 and ends at column line 4?

options.option: grid-column: 2 / 4
options.comment: grid-column: start / end uses grid line numbers. Line 2 to line 4 spans 2 column tracks (tracks 2 and 3). Negative line numbers count from the end.

options.option: grid-column: 2 span 4
column-start: 2; column-end: 4
grid-column-span: 2-4

questions.question: In CSS Grid, what is the difference between justify-items and justify-content?

options.option: justify-items aligns items in cells; justify-content aligns grid tracks in the container
options.comment: justify-items controls how items are aligned inside their individual grid areas. justify-content controls how the grid tracks themselves are distributed when the grid is smaller than the container.

options.option: They are identical in CSS Grid
justify-items applies to rows; justify-content applies to columns
justify-content is from Flexbox and does not work in Grid

questions.question: What does repeat(auto-fill, minmax(200px, 1fr)) do?

options.option: Creates as many columns as fit, each at least 200px wide, filling the container
options.comment: auto-fill automatically creates as many column tracks as possible given the minimum size. Combined with minmax(200px, 1fr) columns are at least 200px but stretch to fill any remaining space — a common responsive pattern.

options.option: Creates exactly 200 columns of 1fr each
Fills the grid with rows of 200px height
Creates a fixed grid that auto-fills with placeholder content

questions.question: What is the difference between auto-fill and auto-fit in repeat()?

options.option: auto-fill keeps empty tracks; auto-fit collapses them so items can stretch
options.comment: When there are fewer items than columns, auto-fill keeps empty tracks (items do not grow beyond their max). auto-fit collapses the empty tracks, allowing remaining items to expand to fill the row.

options.option: auto-fill is for rows; auto-fit is for columns
auto-fit is not a valid keyword — only auto-fill exists
They are identical — the names are interchangeable

questions.question: What does grid-area: 1 / 1 / 3 / 3 mean on a grid item?

options.option: The item spans from row line 1 to row line 3 and column line 1 to column line 3
options.comment: grid-area is shorthand for grid-row-start / grid-column-start / grid-row-end / grid-column-end. This item occupies a 2x2 area starting at the top-left corner of the grid.

options.option: The item is placed in grid area named 1-1-3-3
The item spans 1 row and 1 column starting at position 3,3
The item is 3 columns wide and 3 rows tall

questions.question: What does grid-auto-flow: dense do?

options.option: It backfills gaps by placing smaller items into holes left by larger items
options.comment: dense tells the auto-placement algorithm to try to fill holes earlier in the grid if later items are small enough to fit. This may change visual order from DOM order.

options.option: It makes grid items fill their entire cell with no gaps
It makes the grid tracks as dense as possible by removing gap
It stacks items on top of each other in a single cell

questions.question: An item is placed with grid-column: 1 / -1. What does -1 refer to?

options.option: The last grid line of the explicit column grid
options.comment: Negative line numbers count backwards from the end of the explicit grid. -1 is always the last explicit column line, so grid-column: 1 / -1 spans the full width of the defined grid.

options.option: One column before the first column
An invalid value that causes the item to overflow
The implicit grid area outside the defined columns

questions.question: What problem does the subgrid value (grid-template-columns: subgrid) solve?

options.option: It lets a nested grid item align to the parent grid tracks instead of creating its own
options.comment: Without subgrid, a nested grid creates independent tracks and cannot align to the parent. subgrid inherits the parent's tracks, enabling deep alignment — essential for card grids where inner content must line up across cards.

options.option: It allows a grid to have more than 12 columns
It duplicates all tracks from a sibling grid element
It forces items into a sub-pixel grid for sharper rendering

questions.question: Two grid items are explicitly placed in the same cell. How does the browser render them, and how can you control which is on top?

options.option: Both overlap in the same cell; z-index controls which is on top
options.comment: CSS Grid allows deliberate overlapping by assigning items to the same grid lines. Grid items form stacking contexts, so z-index determines the visual stacking order without needing position: relative.

options.option: The browser only renders the first item; the second is discarded
They are stacked vertically inside the cell
Only position: absolute can create overlap inside a grid

questions.question: What is a CSS media query used for?

options.option: Applying styles conditionally based on device characteristics like screen width
options.comment: Media queries let you apply CSS rules only when certain conditions are met — most commonly a min-width or max-width — enabling responsive layouts that adapt to different screen sizes.

options.option: Fetching external stylesheets over a network
Querying the DOM for elements with a specific class
Playing media files like video and audio

questions.question: What does the viewport meta tag <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1"> do?

options.option: Tells the browser to match the viewport width to the device screen width at 1:1 scale
options.comment: Without this tag, mobile browsers zoom out to fit a desktop layout, making media queries ineffective. This tag is required for responsive design to work correctly on mobile.

options.option: Sets the default font size for the page
Prevents users from zooming in on the page
Defines the maximum width of the page layout

questions.question: What does "mobile-first" mean in responsive design?

options.option: Writing base styles for small screens, then enhancing for larger screens with min-width
options.comment: Mobile-first means the default styles target small screens. min-width media queries progressively enhance the layout for larger screens. This is the opposite of desktop-first (max-width queries).

options.option: Building a separate mobile-only website
Making the mobile version of the site load first in all browsers
Hiding desktop content on mobile devices

questions.question: Which CSS unit is equal to 1% of the viewport width?

options.option: vw
options.comment: 1vw equals 1% of the viewport width. 100vw is the full viewport width. Similarly, vh is 1% of viewport height.

options.option: vh
em
px

questions.question: A media query uses @media (max-width: 768px). When does it apply?

options.option: When the viewport is 768px wide or narrower
options.comment: max-width is a desktop-first approach: styles inside apply when the screen is at most the specified value. For mobile-first, min-width is preferred.

options.option: When the viewport is wider than 768px
When the element has a max-width of 768px
When the page is scrolled to 768px down

questions.question: What makes a layout "fluid" in responsive design?

options.option: Using percentage or flexible units so elements resize proportionally with the viewport
options.comment: Fluid layouts use relative units (%, fr, vw) rather than fixed pixels so elements naturally resize. This is a foundation of responsive design alongside media queries.

options.option: Adding CSS animations to all elements
Using overflow: scroll on the body
Setting all elements to float: left

questions.question: What is a breakpoint in responsive design?

options.option: A viewport width at which the layout changes via a media query
options.comment: Breakpoints are the screen widths where you switch from one layout to another. Common breakpoints are around 480px (mobile), 768px (tablet), and 1024px (desktop).

options.option: A CSS property that breaks a page into multiple sections
A point in the code where execution stops for debugging
A fixed width applied to the body element

questions.question: What does the CSS clamp() function do?

options.option: Sets a value with a minimum, preferred, and maximum — it scales fluidly between the bounds
options.comment: clamp(min, preferred, max) is ideal for fluid typography and spacing. e.g. clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 2rem) grows with the viewport but never goes below 1rem or above 2rem.

options.option: Clamps an element to its parent's boundaries
Prevents overflow by hiding content that exceeds the container
Fixes an element's position at the top of the viewport

questions.question: Why is max-width commonly set on a page wrapper element in responsive design?

options.option: To prevent the layout stretching too wide on large screens while staying fluid on small ones
options.comment: A fluid layout without a max-width would become unreadable on very wide monitors. Setting max-width: 1200px with width: 100% keeps content comfortable on all screen sizes.

options.option: To force the page to scroll horizontally
To disable responsive behavior on desktop screens
To set the default font size for the page

questions.question: What does the srcset attribute on an element do?

options.option: Provides multiple image sources so the browser can pick the most appropriate one for the screen
options.comment: srcset lets you supply different image files for different screen densities or sizes. The browser chooses the best match, reducing bandwidth on small screens.

options.option: Sets the image source using CSS instead of HTML
Loads multiple images simultaneously for a slideshow
Defines the set of CSS classes applied to the image

questions.question: Which approach is preferred for modern responsive design and why?

options.option: min-width (mobile-first) — base styles are minimal; enhancements added for larger screens
options.comment: Mobile-first with min-width means smaller devices download only what they need. Desktop enhancements are progressive additions, which is better for performance and maintainability.

options.option: max-width (desktop-first) because desktops have more capabilities
Using only fixed pixel widths without media queries
Both are equally recommended with no practical difference

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the HTML <picture> element in responsive design?

options.option: It enables art direction — serving a different image crop or format based on media conditions
options.comment: <picture> wraps <source> elements with media queries, letting you swap entire images (not just resize them). Useful for serving a zoomed-in crop on mobile vs. a wide shot on desktop.

options.option: It displays a picture in a modal overlay
It is a replacement for the tag
It compresses images automatically for mobile

questions.question: What problem do CSS container queries solve that media queries cannot?

options.option: They apply styles based on the size of a parent container, not the viewport
options.comment: Media queries respond to the viewport. Container queries (@container) respond to the size of the element's own container, enabling truly component-level responsiveness.

options.option: They apply styles only inside CSS Grid containers
They are faster than media queries at runtime
They allow querying CSS variable values

questions.question: What CSS declaration prevents iOS Safari from automatically enlarging font sizes in landscape mode?

options.option: -webkit-text-size-adjust: 100% (or text-size-adjust: 100%)
options.comment: iOS Safari automatically inflates text in landscape. Setting -webkit-text-size-adjust: 100% (typically on html) disables this, giving you full control over font sizes via media queries.

options.option: font-size: fixed
overflow: hidden on the html element
viewport-font-scale: 1 in the meta tag

questions.question: Why are CSS logical properties (margin-inline-start, padding-block-end, etc.) useful in responsive design?

options.option: They adapt to writing direction automatically, making layouts work in LTR and RTL without extra code
options.comment: Physical properties (margin-left) are tied to a specific direction. Logical properties map to start/end/block/inline and automatically flip in RTL writing modes, simplifying internationalisation.

options.option: They replace media queries for responsive layouts
They are shorter to type than traditional margin and padding
They only apply inside a CSS Grid container

questions.question: What must you do before writing @container rules for an element?

options.option: Set container-type (inline-size or size) on the parent element you want to query
options.comment: container-type establishes the containment context. inline-size tracks width only; size tracks both width and height. Without this, @container queries have nothing to measure.

options.option: Add a container class to the HTML element
Set display: container on the parent element
Nothing — @container works on any element by default

questions.question: Intrinsic web design extends responsive design by doing what?

options.option: Letting content define its own size using CSS Grid, min/max, and clamp instead of only breakpoints
options.comment: Coined by Jen Simmons, intrinsic design means layouts respond to both the viewport and the content inside them. Techniques like minmax(), auto-fill, and clamp reduce reliance on explicit breakpoints.

options.option: Removing all media queries from modern codebases
Using JavaScript to recalculate layouts at runtime
Generating separate HTML files for each screen size

questions.question: In a responsive image with srcset and sizes, what does the sizes attribute tell the browser?

options.option: The expected display size of the image at different viewport widths before layout
options.comment: sizes hints to the browser how large the image will appear on screen (e.g. 50vw at wide viewports, 100vw on mobile). The browser uses this to pick the most efficient source from srcset without waiting for CSS to calculate.

options.option: The file sizes of each image in the srcset
The maximum and minimum width the image element can have
The number of images to load from the srcset

questions.question: Which technique creates typography that scales smoothly between a minimum and maximum size across all viewport widths without breakpoints?

options.option: font-size: clamp(1rem, 1rem + 2vw, 2.5rem)
options.comment: clamp() with a viewport-relative preferred value (1rem + 2vw) creates fluid typography. The font scales with the viewport but is clamped to a minimum of 1rem and maximum of 2.5rem, eliminating the need for font-size breakpoints.

options.option: font-size: responsive(1rem, 2.5rem)
font-scale: fluid in the root element
font-size: auto

questions.question: What is the purpose of the viewport meta tag in responsive design?

options.option: It tells the browser to match viewport width to device screen width at 1:1 scale
options.comment: Without the viewport meta tag, mobile browsers zoom out to fit a desktop layout, making media queries ineffective. It is required for responsive design to work on mobile.

options.option: It sets the default font size for the entire page
It prevents users from zooming in on the page
It defines the maximum width of the page layout

questions.question: What is the difference between a serif and a sans-serif font?

options.option: Serif fonts have small decorative strokes on letters; sans-serif fonts do not
options.comment: Serifs are the small finishing strokes on letterforms (e.g. Times New Roman). Sans-serif fonts lack these (e.g. Arial, Helvetica). Sans-serif is generally preferred on screens for readability.

options.option: Serif fonts are bold by default; sans-serif fonts are not
Sans-serif fonts are only for headings; serif is for body text
Serif fonts require a Google Fonts import; sans-serif are built in

questions.question: What does the em unit represent in CSS typography?

options.option: A multiple of the current element's font-size
options.comment: 1em equals the computed font-size of the element itself. If font-size is 16px, then 1.5em = 24px. When used on font-size itself, em inherits from the parent.

options.option: The height of the capital letter M in the current font
A fixed unit equal to 16 pixels
The viewport width divided by 100

questions.question: Why is a font-family value written as a comma-separated list?

options.option: It defines fallback fonts in case the first choice is unavailable
options.comment: The browser tries each font in order. If the first font fails to load, it falls back to the next, and so on. The list usually ends with a generic family like sans-serif or serif.

options.option: It applies multiple fonts to different characters in the same text
Each font in the list is loaded simultaneously for faster rendering
It overrides font settings inherited from the parent element

questions.question: What color does the hex value #ffffff represent?

options.option: White
options.comment: #ffffff is maximum red, green, and blue (255, 255, 255). All channels at full intensity produce white. #000000 is all channels at zero — black.

options.option: Black
Transparent
Gray

questions.question: What does line-height: 1.5 mean in CSS?

options.option: The space between lines of text is 1.5 times the current font-size
options.comment: A unitless line-height is a multiplier of font-size. 1.5 is a common default for body text readability. Using a unitless value (not 1.5em) prevents unexpected inheritance issues.

options.option: The text is 1.5px tall
There are 1.5 characters per line
The line spacing is 1.5% of the viewport height

questions.question: What does rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.5) define in CSS?

options.option: Black at 50% opacity
options.comment: rgba() takes red, green, blue (0-255) and alpha (0-1). 0,0,0 is black. An alpha of 0.5 makes it 50% transparent, useful for overlays and shadows.

options.option: A dark gray color
Fully transparent black
A color with 0.5% saturation

questions.question: What is the numeric equivalent of font-weight: bold?

options.comment: CSS font-weight numeric scale runs from 100 (thin) to 900 (black). bold maps to 700, and normal maps to 400. Numeric values give finer control for variable fonts.

questions.question: What is the difference between em and rem units in CSS?

options.option: em is relative to the current element's font-size; rem is relative to the root element's font-size
options.comment: rem (root em) always refers to the html element's font-size, making it predictable in nested components. em can compound through nesting, making it harder to reason about.

options.option: em is for font sizes only; rem is for all other measurements
rem is larger than em by a factor of 10
They are identical — rem is just the modern alias for em

questions.question: What do the three values in hsl(210, 100%, 50%) represent?

options.option: Hue (0-360 degrees), saturation (%), and lightness (%)
options.comment: HSL is often more intuitive than hex. Hue is a color wheel angle (0=red, 120=green, 240=blue). Saturation controls color intensity. Lightness controls brightness (0%=black, 100%=white).

options.option: Horizontal, shadow, and luminosity
Highlight, shade, and layer values
Height, spacing, and line-height values

questions.question: Which three CSS declarations are needed together to show an ellipsis (...) when text overflows?

options.option: overflow: hidden; white-space: nowrap; text-overflow: ellipsis
options.comment: text-overflow: ellipsis only works if the text is on a single line (white-space: nowrap) and overflow is hidden. All three are required together.

options.option: display: ellipsis; max-width: 100%; overflow: clip
text-overflow: ellipsis alone is sufficient
white-space: ellipsis; overflow: auto; clip: text

questions.question: What does font-display: swap do in a @font-face declaration?

options.option: Shows a fallback font immediately, then swaps to the custom font once it loads
options.comment: swap prevents invisible text during font load (FOIT). The browser shows a fallback instantly, then replaces it when the custom font is ready — causing a brief Flash of Unstyled Text (FOUT).

options.option: Swaps the font to italic style on hover
Preloads all font weights simultaneously
Delays page render until the font finishes loading

questions.question: What does the currentColor keyword do in CSS?

options.option: It inherits the element's computed color value for use in other properties
options.comment: currentColor resolves to the inherited or declared color property. Useful for border-color, background-color, or SVG fill when you want them to match the text color automatically.

options.option: It returns the browser's default text color
It switches the color based on the user's OS theme
It references the last color value declared in the stylesheet

questions.question: Why do developers sometimes set html { font-size: 62.5% }?

options.option: To make 1rem equal 10px, simplifying rem-based calculations
options.comment: The default font-size is 16px. 62.5% of 16px = 10px. With this base, 1.6rem = 16px, 2.4rem = 24px — making rem math intuitive. A modern alternative is to use calc() or just work with 16px as the base.

options.option: To match the default system font size on all operating systems
To reduce the font size to 62.5% of the parent's value
To disable browser default font-size overrides

questions.question: What does the CSS color(display-p3 ...) syntax enable over standard hex/RGB?

options.option: Access to the wider Display P3 color gamut, which includes more vivid colors than sRGB
options.comment: Standard CSS colors are limited to sRGB. The P3 color space covers ~25% more colors — especially more vibrant greens and reds — visible on modern displays like Apple Retina and OLED screens.

options.option: Converts color values to grayscale automatically
Displays color values as percentages instead of integers
Adds a transparency channel to any color value

questions.question: What is a variable font, and which CSS property controls its axes?

options.option: A font with multiple styles in one file; font-variation-settings controls axes like weight and width
options.comment: Variable fonts embed a continuous design space. font-variation-settings lets you set named axes ('wght', 'wdth', 'ital') to any point on the range, reducing HTTP requests vs. loading separate font files.

options.option: A font that changes based on viewport size automatically
A Google Fonts feature that applies random font styles
A system font that adapts to the user's language setting

questions.question: How do CSS custom properties enable consistent color theming across a codebase?

options.option: Define colors once on :root as --var-name; reference everywhere with var(--var-name)
options.comment: Custom properties cascade like normal CSS. Defining --color-primary: #0055ff on :root and using var(--color-primary) throughout means changing the theme is a single-line edit.

options.option: CSS custom properties automatically generate a color palette from a base hue
They work only inside @media queries for theming
They replace hex codes with human-readable names at compile time

questions.question: What minimum contrast ratio does WCAG AA require between normal text and its background?

options.comment: WCAG 2.1 Level AA requires a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 for normal text (below 18pt) and 3:1 for large text (18pt+ or 14pt bold). Level AAA requires 7:1 for normal text.

questions.question: What is the difference between kerning and letter-spacing in CSS typography?

options.option: Kerning adjusts space between specific character pairs; letter-spacing adds uniform space to all
options.comment: font-kerning: auto lets the font's built-in kerning tables adjust pairs like AV or To. letter-spacing (tracking) adds or removes a fixed amount globally, useful for uppercase labels or headings.

options.option: Kerning applies to numbers only; letter-spacing to letters
They are the same — kerning is the older CSS term
Letter-spacing only works on monospace fonts

questions.question: What does the optical size (opsz) axis in a variable font control?

options.option: Fine details like stroke contrast that should change at different display sizes for readability
options.comment: Optical sizing adjusts letter proportions for the size at which the font is displayed. At small sizes, strokes are thickened and spacing is more open; at large display sizes, finer detail is introduced.

options.option: The DPI scaling factor applied to the font on retina screens
The number of pixels rendered per character
The zoom level of the browser viewport

questions.question: What does the CSS color-mix() function do?

options.option: Blends two colors together in a specified color space and at a given ratio
options.comment: color-mix(in srgb, blue 40%, white) produces a color that is 40% blue and 60% white mixed in the sRGB color space. It is useful for generating tints and shades without a preprocessor.

options.option: Converts a color to its complementary hue on the color wheel
Applies a gradient between two colors as a background
Mixes the background and foreground colors using CSS blend modes

questions.question: What does the CSS transition property do?

options.option: Animates a CSS property change smoothly over a specified duration
options.comment: transition: property duration easing delay. For example, transition: opacity 0.3s ease makes opacity changes animate smoothly instead of jumping instantly.

options.option: Moves an element from one position to another
Creates a looping animation with keyframes
Applies a filter effect to an element

questions.question: What does transform: translateX(50px) do to an element?

options.option: Moves the element 50px to the right visually without affecting document flow
options.comment: translateX shifts the element along the X axis. Unlike position or margin, transform does not affect layout — other elements remain in their original position.

options.option: Moves the element and all siblings 50px to the right
Sets the left CSS property to 50px
Scales the element to 50% of its original width

questions.question: What does transform: scale(2) do?

options.option: Doubles the size of the element visually without affecting layout
options.comment: scale() resizes the element around its transform-origin (default center). Like all transforms, it does not push other elements away — the original space is preserved in the document flow.

options.option: Doubles the element's width and height in the document layout
Makes the element twice as heavy in terms of DOM weight
Sets font-size to 2em on the element

questions.question: What does the @keyframes rule define in CSS?

options.option: The intermediate states of an animation at specific points during its duration
options.comment: @keyframes name { from { } to { } } or percentage stops define exactly what the element looks like at each stage. The animation property then applies the keyframe sequence to an element.

options.option: The breakpoints where layout changes in responsive design
The frames per second rate of an animation
The key CSS properties that cannot be animated

questions.question: What does animation-iteration-count: infinite do?

options.option: The animation repeats continuously without stopping
options.comment: infinite makes the animation loop forever. You can also specify a number like 3 to play it exactly 3 times. Use with animation-direction: alternate to reverse on each cycle.

options.option: The animation plays for an infinitely long duration
The animation runs with the maximum possible frames
The animation applies to all children infinitely deep

questions.question: What is the key difference between a CSS transition and a CSS animation?

options.option: Transitions react to state changes; animations run independently with @keyframes
options.comment: A transition plays when a property value changes (e.g., on :hover). A CSS animation runs on its own timeline defined by @keyframes and can loop, have multiple stages, and play automatically.

options.option: Transitions require JavaScript; animations are pure CSS
Animations only work on text; transitions work on any element
Transitions are faster than animations by default

questions.question: What does transform: rotate(45deg) do to an element?

options.option: Rotates the element 45 degrees clockwise around its transform-origin
options.comment: Positive degrees rotate clockwise. Negative degrees rotate counterclockwise. The default transform-origin is the center of the element.

options.option: Rotates the element 45 degrees counterclockwise
Skews the element 45 degrees along the X axis
Rotates the element's text by 45 degrees only

questions.question: What does animation-fill-mode: forwards do?

options.option: The element retains the styles from the last keyframe after the animation ends
options.comment: Without forwards, the element snaps back to its original styles after the animation completes. forwards holds the final keyframe state. backwards applies the initial keyframe during the delay.

options.option: The animation plays forward (left-to-right) instead of in reverse
The element's styles apply before the animation starts
The animation forwards its state to child elements

questions.question: What does the ease-in-out timing function do in a transition or animation?

options.option: Starts slow, speeds up in the middle, then slows again at the end
options.comment: ease-in-out mimics natural motion. ease-in starts slow then accelerates. ease-out decelerates to the end. linear is constant speed. cubic-bezier() lets you define a custom curve.

options.option: Starts fast and ends fast with a slow middle section
Plays the first half forward and the second half in reverse
Bounces at the start and at the end of the animation

questions.question: What is the purpose of the CSS will-change property?

options.option: It hints to the browser which properties will change so it can optimize rendering ahead of time
options.comment: will-change: transform tells the browser to promote the element to its own compositor layer before the animation starts, preventing jank. Use sparingly — overuse wastes GPU memory.

options.option: It prevents an element from changing its CSS properties
It applies a change to an element before the page loads
It specifies which properties a CSS variable can update

questions.question: Why is animating transform and opacity preferred over animating top/left/width/height?

options.option: Transform and opacity are composited on the GPU without triggering layout or paint
options.comment: Changing top/left/width/height triggers expensive layout recalculation and repaint. transform and opacity changes are handled entirely by the compositor on the GPU, resulting in smooth 60fps animations.

options.option: top/left animations do not work on mobile browsers
transform animations are ignored by screen readers
top/left only work with position: absolute elements

questions.question: What does the transform-origin property control?

options.option: The point around which a transform (rotate, scale, skew) is applied
options.comment: Default is 50% 50% (center). Setting transform-origin: top left makes a rotation spin from the top-left corner. Useful for fold animations, card flips, and accordion effects.

options.option: The starting position of a translateX/Y animation
The anchor point for position: absolute elements
The origin CSS file from which the transform was imported

questions.question: What does animation-direction: alternate do?

options.option: Odd iterations play forward; even iterations play in reverse
options.comment: alternate creates a smooth back-and-forth effect. Combined with animation-iteration-count: infinite, the animation plays forward then reverses continuously, great for pulsing or breathing effects.

options.option: The animation alternates between two @keyframes sets
The animation plays forward on desktop and reverse on mobile
The animation direction is chosen randomly each iteration

questions.question: What does the perspective property do when applied to a parent element?

options.option: It defines the distance from the viewer to the z=0 plane, creating a 3D depth effect on children
options.comment: perspective: 800px on a parent makes 3D transforms on children (rotateY, translateZ) appear to recede into or emerge from the screen. Smaller values create more extreme 3D distortion.

options.option: It makes the element semi-transparent
It sets a vanishing point for text perspective effects only
It enables 3D rendering for the entire page

questions.question: What does animation-composition: add do compared to the default replace?

options.option: It adds the animation's effect on top of the underlying property value instead of replacing it
options.comment: With replace (default), the animation overrides the element's value. With add, the animated value is added to the existing one. Useful when multiple animations affect the same property without the values overwriting each other.

options.option: It adds a second animation to the element's animation list
It composites the element onto a separate GPU layer
It sums the durations of all running animations

questions.question: What does animation-timeline: scroll() enable in CSS?

options.option: The animation progresses based on scroll position instead of time
options.comment: Scroll-driven animations (animation-timeline: scroll()) link @keyframes to scroll progress. As the user scrolls, the animation advances — no JavaScript needed for parallax, progress bars, or reveal effects.

options.option: Makes the animation play only while the user is scrolling
Scrolls the page automatically to trigger the animation
Applies a scroll-snap effect to the animated element

questions.question: What does the steps(4, end) timing function do in a CSS animation?

options.option: Divides the animation into 4 discrete jumps, changing only at the end of each interval
options.comment: steps() creates frame-by-frame animation. steps(4, end) jumps at 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100% of the duration. Used for sprite sheet animations where you want instant state changes, not smooth transitions.

options.option: Slows down the animation in 4 stages with increasing delay
Applies the easing function 4 times over the duration
Creates 4 bounce effects at the end of the animation

questions.question: What does the CSS offset-path property enable?

options.option: Moving an element along a custom SVG path or geometric shape over time
options.comment: offset-path is part of CSS Motion Path. Combined with offset-distance animated via @keyframes, an element can travel along any SVG path, circle, or ray — enabling curve-following animations.

options.option: Setting a clipping mask that follows an SVG path
Defining the path CSS property values can travel during a transition
Offsetting the element from its parent's path layout

questions.question: What does the CSS View Transitions API enable?

options.option: Animated transitions between different DOM states or page navigations with a simple API
options.comment: document.startViewTransition() takes a DOM update callback. The browser automatically cross-fades old and new states. You can customise transitions per element using ::view-transition-old and ::view-transition-new pseudo-elements.

options.option: Transitions that only work in fullscreen view mode
Animating the viewport when the user resizes the browser
A polyfill for CSS transitions on older browsers

questions.question: Why should CSS animations respect prefers-reduced-motion: reduce?

options.option: Some users have vestibular disorders where motion causes nausea or dizziness
options.comment: @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) is set when the user enables "reduce motion" in their OS accessibility settings. Disabling or simplifying animations for these users is an accessibility requirement under WCAG 2.3.

options.option: Animations are not allowed on mobile devices by default
Reducing motion improves animation frame rates on all devices
The browser ignores @keyframes unless this setting is detected

questions.question: What does the :hover pseudo-class target?

options.option: An element when the user's pointer is positioned over it
options.comment: :hover applies styles when the mouse cursor is over the element. It is commonly used on links, buttons, and cards to provide visual feedback.

options.option: An element when it receives keyboard focus
The first element the user clicked on the page
Any element that contains a link

questions.question: What does li:nth-child(2) select?

options.option: The second li element within its parent
options.comment: :nth-child(n) selects the element that is the nth child of its parent. li:nth-child(2) selects an li if it is the second child of its parent. Counting starts from 1.

options.option: Every second li element on the page
The last li in any list
Any li that has at least 2 children

questions.question: What does the ::before pseudo-element insert?

options.option: A generated content element as the first child of the selected element
options.comment: ::before creates a virtual first child. It requires the content property (even if empty). Commonly used for decorative icons, quotes, or clearing floats without extra HTML.

options.option: HTML content before the element in the DOM
A tooltip that appears when hovering before the element
Styles that apply only before the page loads

questions.question: What is the difference between a pseudo-class and a pseudo-element?

options.option: Pseudo-classes target element states; pseudo-elements create virtual parts of an element
options.comment: Pseudo-classes style based on state or position in the tree. Pseudo-elements create new "virtual" nodes that you can style — like the first line of text or generated content.

options.option: Pseudo-elements use a single colon; pseudo-classes use double colon
Pseudo-classes only work on links; pseudo-elements work on any element
They are the same concept with different naming conventions

questions.question: What does the adjacent sibling combinator (+) select?

options.option: The element that immediately follows a specified element and shares the same parent
options.comment: h2 + p selects a p element that directly follows an h2. Both must share the same parent. If another element appears between them, the rule does not apply.

options.option: All siblings of the specified element
All descendants of the specified element
An element that is a child and also an adjacent element

questions.question: What does the CSS selector input[type="text"] target?

options.option: All input elements with a type attribute equal to "text"
options.comment: Attribute selectors match elements based on their HTML attributes. [type="text"] is an exact match. [attr*="val"] contains, [attr^="val"] starts-with, [attr$="val"] ends-with.

options.option: All input elements regardless of type
Any element containing the text "text"
Input elements inside a form with type attribute

questions.question: What does the general sibling combinator (~) select?

options.option: All subsequent siblings of a specified element that share the same parent
options.comment: h2 ~ p selects all p elements that follow an h2 inside the same parent — not just the immediately adjacent one. The + combinator only gets the first following sibling.

options.option: Only the immediately following sibling element
All elements at the same nesting level in the entire document
Elements that have an approximately similar relationship

questions.question: What does the :not() pseudo-class do?

options.option: Selects elements that do NOT match the selector inside the parentheses
options.comment: li:not(.active) selects all li elements that do not have the class active. In modern CSS, :not() accepts complex selectors including multiple arguments: :not(.a, .b).

options.option: Removes matching elements from the DOM
Prevents the element from inheriting styles
Selects elements that are not currently visible

questions.question: When does :focus-visible apply, and why is it preferred over :focus for keyboard accessibility?

options.option: It shows a focus ring only for keyboard navigation, not mouse clicks
options.comment: :focus always applies when an element receives focus. :focus-visible applies only when the browser determines focus should be visible (keyboard nav). This prevents distracting outlines on mouse clicks while keeping them for keyboard users.

options.option: It makes the focus indicator always visible even without keyboard interaction
It is the same as :focus but with a higher specificity
It applies when the element has both :hover and :focus simultaneously

questions.question: What does :nth-child(2n+1) select?

options.option: All odd-numbered children (1st, 3rd, 5th, ...)
options.comment: :nth-child(2n+1) is equivalent to :nth-child(odd). The formula An+B: A is the cycle size, B is the offset. 2n+1 hits positions 1, 3, 5... Useful for zebra-stripe table rows.

options.option: Every second child starting from the third
The 2nd and 1st children only
Children at positions that are multiples of 2 plus 1

questions.question: What does the :has() relational pseudo-class enable?

options.option: Selecting a parent element based on what children or siblings it contains
options.comment: div:has(img) selects div elements that contain an img. This was impossible before :has() — CSS had no "parent selector." It is now widely supported and transforms how complex selectors are written.

options.option: Checking whether an element has a specific CSS property set
Selecting elements that have more than one class
Testing if an element has any inline styles applied

questions.question: What does the :is() pseudo-class do?

options.option: Matches any element in the list and takes the highest specificity of the arguments
options.comment: :is(h1, h2, h3) is equivalent to h1, h2, h3 but in a compact form. It also takes the specificity of its most specific argument, which is different from :where() which has zero specificity.

options.option: Checks whether an element is a certain HTML tag
Applies styles only when JavaScript confirms a condition is true
Creates an alias for a long selector string

questions.question: In CSS specificity, which selector type has the highest weight?

options.option: Inline styles (applied via the style attribute on the HTML element)
options.comment: Specificity weight (highest to lowest): inline styles (1,0,0,0), IDs (0,1,0,0), classes/attributes/pseudo-classes (0,0,1,0), elements/pseudo-elements (0,0,0,1). !important overrides all.

options.option: ID selectors (#id)
The !important keyword in any selector
Class selectors (.class)

questions.question: What is the difference between :where() and :is() in CSS?

options.option: :where() always has zero specificity; :is() takes the specificity of its arguments
options.comment: :where(h1, h2) and :is(h1, h2) match the same elements, but :where() contributes nothing to specificity. This makes :where() ideal for base styles that should be easily overridden.

options.option: :where() only works with element selectors; :is() accepts all selector types
They are identical — :where() is just an alias for :is()
:is() is the newer replacement and :where() is deprecated

questions.question: What does :nth-child(2 of .highlight) select?

options.option: The second element with class .highlight among its siblings
options.comment: The "of S" syntax in :nth-child() filters by a selector first, then applies the nth count only among matching elements. Without "of", :nth-child counts ALL siblings regardless of type.

options.option: The 2nd child of any element with class .highlight
Every other element that has the .highlight class
The element at position 2 in a CSS Grid with .highlight class

questions.question: CSS selectors are evaluated right-to-left by the browser. What does this mean for performance?

options.option: The browser first finds all matches for the rightmost part, then filters up the tree
options.comment: For div .item span, the browser finds all span elements first, then checks if a parent has .item, then if that is inside a div. Writing specific key selectors (the rightmost part) avoids large initial match sets.

options.option: Selectors are evaluated from top to bottom in the stylesheet
Selectors are only evaluated when JavaScript modifies the DOM
The last rule in the stylesheet is applied first

questions.question: What does the :scope pseudo-class represent?

options.option: The reference point element — e.g. the element passed to querySelectorAll
options.comment: In a stylesheet :scope refers to the root (:root). In JavaScript, element.querySelectorAll(":scope > div") matches direct child divs of that specific element. Also used in @scope rules to limit styles to a subtree.

options.option: The currently focused element in the document
An element that has been explicitly given a CSS scope declaration
The body element of the page

questions.question: What does the @scope CSS rule do?

options.option: Limits style rules to apply only within a defined subtree, with an optional lower boundary
options.comment: @scope (.card) { ... } scopes rules to .card descendants. @scope (.card) to (.card__footer) excludes the footer subtree. This avoids class name collisions between components without BEM or Shadow DOM.

options.option: Enables JavaScript variable scope to be used in CSS
Creates a scoped stylesheet that only applies to one HTML element
Defines the specificity scope for a group of selectors

questions.question: What does form:has(input:not(:placeholder-shown):invalid) select?

options.option: A form that contains an input with a value entered but that value fails validation
options.comment: :not(:placeholder-shown) means the field has user input (placeholder hidden). :invalid means the value fails the constraint. Together this targets "filled but invalid" — a common pattern for showing error states only after the user has typed.

options.option: A form where all inputs are empty and invalid
An input that is invalid and has no placeholder attribute
A form that does not have a placeholder and is not valid

questions.question: What specificity does :has(.highlight) contribute?

options.option: One class (0,0,1,0) — :has() takes the specificity of its most specific argument
options.comment: :has(.highlight) contributes the specificity of .highlight which is one class (0,0,1,0). This is the same rule as :is() — unlike :where() which always contributes zero specificity.

options.option: Zero specificity — relational pseudo-classes have no weight
One pseudo-class + one class (0,0,2,0)
Specificity depends on the depth of the element tree

questions.question: What does BEM stand for in CSS architecture?

options.option: Block, Element, Modifier
options.comment: BEM is a naming methodology: Block (.card), Element (.card__title), Modifier (.card--featured). It creates explicit, collision-free class names that communicate structure in the name itself.

options.option: Base, Extension, Module
Border, Element, Margin
Block, Export, Method

questions.question: How do you declare and use a CSS custom property (variable)?

options.option: Declare with --name: value on a selector; use with var(--name)
options.comment: CSS custom properties start with --. They cascade and inherit like regular properties. Declaring on :root makes them globally available. var(--color-primary) reads the value wherever it is used.

options.option: Declare with $name: value; use with $name
Declare with @variable name: value; use with @name
Declare with const name = value in CSS; use with {name}

questions.question: What is the CSS cascade?

options.option: The algorithm that determines which styles apply when multiple rules target the same element
options.comment: The cascade resolves conflicts by weighing origin (author vs. user vs. browser), importance (!important), specificity, and source order. Understanding it is essential to writing predictable CSS.

options.option: A technique for making elements fall down the page on scroll
A way to inherit font and color properties from parent elements
The order in which CSS files are loaded by the browser

questions.question: Which of the following CSS properties is inherited by default?

options.option: color
options.comment: Inherited properties pass to children automatically: color, font-*, line-height, text-align. Layout/box properties like margin, padding, border, width do not inherit by default. Use inherit to force inheritance on any property.

options.option: margin
border
padding

questions.question: What is the purpose of a CSS reset or normalize stylesheet?

options.option: To remove or standardize inconsistent default browser styles
options.comment: Different browsers apply different default margins, paddings, and styles. A reset (Eric Meyer's) strips all defaults to zero. Normalize.css preserves useful defaults while harmonising inconsistencies across browsers.

options.option: To reset all custom styles back to browser defaults
To clear the browser cache before loading a page
To prevent CSS files from being loaded in the wrong order

questions.question: What does the !important declaration do in CSS?

options.option: Forces a declaration to override all other conflicting rules regardless of specificity
options.comment: !important breaks the normal cascade. It should be used sparingly — typically only in utility classes or overriding third-party CSS — because it makes specificity conflicts much harder to debug.

options.option: Marks a CSS property as required for the page to render
Prevents a CSS property from being overridden by JavaScript
Makes a CSS rule apply on all elements of the page

questions.question: What is the utility-first CSS approach (as used in Tailwind CSS)?

options.option: Building UI by composing small single-purpose utility classes in HTML
options.comment: Instead of writing custom CSS, you apply pre-built utility classes like p-4, text-center, flex. This reduces CSS bundle growth and keeps styles close to the markup they affect.

options.option: Writing CSS utilities in JavaScript for server-side rendering
Using only native browser utility APIs for styling
Generating CSS automatically from utility functions in JavaScript

questions.question: In BEM, what does the double underscore (__) signify in a class name like .card__title?

options.option: .title is an Element — a part that belongs to the .card Block
options.comment: The Block__Element pattern shows ownership. .card__title is a title that is semantically part of the card. An element should only exist within its block context.

options.option: .title is a child block nested inside .card
It is a modified variant of the .card component
It is an ID selector written in BEM style

questions.question: What problem does @layer solve in CSS architecture?

options.option: Explicit cascade layer order so low-specificity utilities can override high-specificity components
options.comment: @layer lets you declare cascade layers and their precedence. Rules in a later layer win regardless of specificity. @layer base, components, utilities means utilities always beat components even with lower specificity selectors.

options.option: Splitting a large CSS file into multiple layers for parallel loading
Grouping CSS rules by the DOM layer (z-index) they apply to
Allowing CSS files to be imported in any order without conflicts

questions.question: What is the main benefit of CSS-in-JS libraries (like styled-components)?

options.option: Styles are scoped to the component by default, eliminating class name collisions
options.comment: CSS-in-JS generates unique class names per component, so .button in one component cannot clash with .button in another. It also enables dynamic styles based on props without extra class toggling.

options.option: CSS-in-JS is faster than regular CSS in all browsers
It compiles CSS at the OS level for maximum performance
It removes the need for a build tool or bundler

questions.question: What does native CSS nesting allow you to do?

options.option: Write child selectors and pseudo-classes inside a parent rule without repeating the parent selector
options.comment: Nesting mirrors Sass/SCSS syntax. .card { &:hover { } .card__title { } } is equivalent to separate .card:hover and .card .card__title rules. It ships in all major browsers as of 2024.

options.option: Create deeply nested HTML structures using CSS only
Import one CSS file inside another at build time
Apply styles to nested flex or grid items directly

questions.question: Which CSS architecture principle does the BEM Modifier (.block--modifier) follow?

options.option: Open/closed: extend component appearance by adding a modifier class, not by editing base styles
options.comment: The base .btn class is closed to modification. .btn--danger adds a variation without touching the base. This prevents unintended regressions elsewhere when a component variant is added.

options.option: DRY: a modifier removes duplicate style declarations
Separation of concerns: modifiers separate CSS from JavaScript
Encapsulation: modifiers prevent styles from leaking to child elements

questions.question: What does contain: layout paint do on an element?

options.option: Isolates the element so its layout and painting changes do not affect the rest of the document
options.comment: CSS containment limits the scope of browser recalculations. contain: layout means the element's box does not affect siblings. contain: paint means nothing outside the box is painted inside. Critical for performance in large UIs.

options.option: Prevents CSS custom properties from being inherited inside the element
Locks the element's dimensions to its initial paint size
Paints the element on a separate hardware layer automatically

questions.question: What is the core idea of the ITCSS (Inverted Triangle CSS) methodology?

options.option: Organising CSS from generic/low-specificity settings to specific/high-specificity components
options.comment: ITCSS arranges CSS in layers from wide reach and low specificity (settings, tools, generic) to narrow reach and high specificity (components, utilities). This prevents specificity wars by ensuring overrides are always further down the triangle.

options.option: Inverting all CSS property values to their opposites for dark mode
Writing CSS selectors in reverse order for performance
A method to load CSS files from the bottom of the HTML up

questions.question: In var(--color, var(--fallback, blue)), when does blue apply?

options.option: When both --color and --fallback are not defined or are invalid
options.comment: var() accepts a fallback as the second argument. If --color is unset, it tries var(--fallback). If that too is unset, it uses blue. Fallbacks can be nested, enabling safe progressive enhancement.

options.option: When the user's browser does not support CSS variables
When the element has no parent with a matching custom property
When --color is set to transparent

questions.question: How does Shadow DOM provide CSS encapsulation different from BEM?

options.option: Shadow DOM is browser-enforced; BEM is a naming convention with no style boundary enforcement
options.comment: BEM prevents collisions by naming discipline — but nothing stops a global rule from breaking a BEM component. Shadow DOM's style boundary is enforced by the browser: outside styles cannot pierce in, and internal styles cannot leak out (except inherited properties).

options.option: Shadow DOM uses inline styles while BEM uses class names
They are identical — Shadow DOM is the browser implementation of BEM
BEM provides stronger isolation because it uses ID-level specificity

questions.question: What does the Single Responsibility Principle mean when applied to CSS classes?

options.option: Each class should do one thing — changing it should only affect that one concern
options.comment: A class like .visually-hidden only handles screen-reader visibility. A class like .align-center only handles text alignment. This makes classes composable, predictable, and easy to debug.

options.option: Each HTML element should only have one CSS class applied to it
A CSS file should export only one component's styles
Only one developer should be responsible for modifying a CSS file

questions.question: A rule in @layer utilities beats a rule in @layer components even if the components rule has higher specificity. Why?

options.option: Later layers always win over earlier layers regardless of selector specificity
options.comment: @layer resolves conflicts at the layer level first. Since utilities is declared after components, all utilities rules beat all components rules regardless of selector specificity. Specificity only matters within the same layer.

options.option: Utility classes always have !important applied implicitly
The @layer rule gives all its contents a higher specificity weight
CSS specificity does not apply inside @layer blocks

questions.question: How do design tokens improve maintainability when implemented as CSS custom properties?

options.option: Token names describe purpose not value; changing one token updates everywhere it is used
options.comment: --color-action: #0055ff is a token. Components reference --color-action, not #0055ff directly. Changing the token updates every component using it. This separates design decisions from implementation.

options.option: Design tokens auto-generate CSS files from a design tool like Figma
They replace the need for a CSS preprocessor by compiling at build time
Custom properties allow design tokens to be read by JavaScript at runtime

questions.question: In native CSS nesting, what does the & symbol represent?

options.option: A reference to the parent selector, used to construct combined or modified selectors
options.comment: .card { &:hover { } &--featured { } } — & is replaced by .card. &:hover becomes .card:hover. &--featured becomes .card--featured. Without &, a nested selector implicitly becomes a descendant.

options.option: A logical AND operator combining two selectors
An escape character for special characters in class names
A reference to the :root element in nested rules
